Metadata.Case_Number: [2025] SGHC 27; Bankruptcy No 2648 of 2018; Summonses Nos 4314, 4315, 4316 of 2022; Originating Application No 797 of 2022
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: SME Care Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: In 2012, SME was a licensed money lender.
In 2012, JASC Pte Ltd obtained a loan from SME, secured by mortgages and a personal guarantee by Ms Chan.
The repayment date of the loan was extended thrice.
In 2014, JASC failed to make full repayment of the disbursed loan amount, outstanding late interest fees, late payment processing fees and extension fees.
In 2014, JASC applied in OS 850/2014 to set aside the loan agreement or to revise the interest rate under the Loan.
In September 2016, SME brought Suit 995/2016 against Ms Chan to enforce the personal guarantee given by her.
On 26 September 2017, a settlement agreement was entered into between SME and Ms Chan in respect of Suit 995.
On 26 September 2017, Ms Chan signed a consent judgment, which SME could enter judgment for in the event of Ms Chan's breach of the Settlement Agreement.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a creditor's proof of debt based on a consent judgment should be admitted in bankruptcy proceedings.
Party_Details.Rule: In bankruptcy administration, a creditor must prove its debt on a balance of probabilities, and a trustee examines proofs of debt and may go behind underlying documents, including judgments and settlements, where there is a reasonable basis to question whether the debt is genuinely due.
Party_Details.Application: The creditor submitted a proof of debt based on a prior consent judgment. The dispute centers on whether the bankruptcy trustee or the court should go behind the consent judgment to reassess the validity and interest structure of the underlying loan.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge held that the proof of debt was founded on a valid and subsisting consent judgment and that a consent order is binding until set aside in separate proceedings. The court considered the principles on going behind judgments in the proof of debt context, including the need for indications of fraud, collusion or miscarriage of justice. The judge noted that earlier proceedings (SME Care Pte Ltd v Chan Siew Lee Jannie [2018] SGHC 96) had already addressed and rejected allegations of fraud or misrepresentation in relation to the settlement agreement. In this application, the bankrupt's further assertions about pressure, lack of legal representation and not reading the documents were found not to indicate fraud, undue influence or misrepresentation. The judge observed that the private trustee had in fact gone behind the consent judgment to examine the underlying loan, interest rates, and the moneylending transaction, and concluded that the loan, as reopened and re-rated in OS 850/2014, was valid. The court accepted the trustee's approach of rejecting compound interest and admitting only simple interest. The judge rejected arguments that the transaction was an extortionate credit transaction under section 103 of the Bankruptcy Act, noting that the loan was between SME and JASC with the bankrupt as guarantor, and that the interest had already been judicially revised. The court also rejected contentions of illegality arising from compound interest, given the trustee's adjustment and the permissibility of contractual interest under section 12(2)(b) of the Civil Law Act. Alleged non-compliance with Bankruptcy Rules in the filing of the proof was found either unfounded or irrelevant because the proof was predicated on the consent judgment rather than the original loan agreement. The judge concluded that there was no basis to go behind the consent judgment further and no basis to invalidate SME's admitted claim, and therefore dismissed the bankrupt's application to reverse the trustee's partial admission.

Metadata.Case_Number: [2025] SGHC 27; Bankruptcy No 2648 of 2018; Summonses Nos 4314, 4315, 4316 of 2022; Originating Application No 797 of 2022
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Fulcrum Distressed Partners Limited

Facts.Text: Timor Global Pte Ltd was incorporated in Singapore on 16 February 2005.
From its incorporation until around 15 August 2016, Timor Global Pte Ltd had three directors, including Jannie Chan Siew Lee, Lay Ni Suig Bobby and Tan Tjo Tek.
On 15 August 2016, Tan Tjo Tek resigned as a director of Timor Global Pte Ltd, leaving Jannie Chan Siew Lee and Lay Ni Suig Bobby as the remaining directors.
Since its incorporation, Jannie Chan Siew Lee was the sole shareholder of Timor Global Pte Ltd.
The Bankrupt was also a 70% shareholder of Timor Global (TL) Pte Ltd, a company registered in Timor Leste. Mr Lay is the sole director of TL.

On 1 July 2007, Timor Global Pte Ltd, Timor Global (TL) Pte Ltd and Intraco Trading Pte Ltd entered into a joint venture agreement for the trading and processing of coffee beans, under which Intraco would advance funds for raw materials to Timor Global (TL) Pte Ltd, resell processed coffee beans to Timor Global Pte Ltd, and share gross profits with Timor Global Pte Ltd according to agreed ratios.

Between 31 July 2007 and 17 October 2007, Intraco Trading Pte Ltd transferred a series of payments totaling approximately S$8.64 million to Timor Global Pte Ltd, and Timor Global Pte Ltd made outgoing payments totaling approximately S$8.14 million to Timor Global (TL) Pte Ltd over multiple dates in that period.
Between 31 July 2007 and 17 October 2007, Intraco Trading Pte Ltd made payments to TGPL totalling S$8,638,389.92, and TGPL made outgoing payments of S$8,142,398.70 to TL.
As at 31 December 2006, Timor Global Pte Ltd's accounts recorded S$6,662,009.44 as amounts due from Timor Global (TL) Pte Ltd.
As at 31 December 2005, Timor Global Pte Ltd's accounts recorded around S$3,111,473.32 as amounts due from Timor Global (TL) Pte Ltd.
In 2008, Timor Global Pte Ltd's balance sheet reflected a net deficit of S$667,000, and its liabilities exceeded its assets from 2008 onwards.
Between 19 June 2008 and 14 November 2008, end-buyers of coffee beans paid S$3,161,247.39 in sales proceeds directly to Timor Global (TL) Pte Ltd instead of Timor Global Pte Ltd.

On 31 December 2008, Timor Global Pte Ltd's general journal recorded entries showing that receivables from various third-party customers were reclassified as amounts due from or assumed by Jannie Chan Siew Lee.
On or around 23 September 2009, Intraco Trading Pte Ltd commenced arbitration proceedings against both TGPL and TL.
On 21 September 2010, Intraco reached a settlement with TL, but continued to pursue its claims against TGPL.
In 2013, TGPL had lent TL various monies over the years and the total amount owing from TL was S$15,343,006.93.

In 2013, during examination of judgment debtor proceedings, Jannie Chan Siew Lee testified that TGPL had lent TL various monies over the years and that the total amount owing was S$15,343,006.93; she also described the purported loans as a cash loan, secured by a director, interest free and repayable on demand.
As at 31 December 2014, Timor Global Pte Ltd's accounts recorded S$15,766,459.98 as total receivables due from Timor Global (TL) Pte Ltd.
In 2014, TGPL's balance sheet records a net deficit of around S$6,512,000.
On 10 March 2015, Intraco obtained an arbitration award of S$6,635,566.93 against TGPL.
On 26 November 2015, Intraco obtained a second arbitration award of S$3,630,001.74 against TGPL.
The Purported Repayment refers to a sum of S$1,276,800 paid from TGPL's bank account to the Bankrupt in August 2007, recorded as a 'loan repayment' to the Bankrupt.

There is a letter dated 31 December 2008 plainly stating that the Bankrupt confirmed that she was assuming the debts of US$16,422.09 owed by David G. Brice to TGPL as at 31 December 2008, with the Bankrupt's signature, corroborated by a general journal entry.

There is a letter dated 31 December 2008 plainly stating that the Bankrupt confirmed that she was assuming the debts of US$16,422.09 owed by David G. Brice to TGPL as at 31 December 2008, with the Bankrupt's signature.
TGPL was wound up on 2 March 2018.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether claims assigned from a company against its former director for alleged unliquidated breaches of fiduciary duty constitute provable debts in bankruptcy.
Party_Details.Rule: In bankruptcy, a claim is provable if it is a liquidated claim or an unliquidated claim arising from specified legal events such as contract, of trust or tort. Claims for of duty by a director can be treated as unliquidated claims arising by reason of of trust and may be resolved through the proof of debt process where appropriate. A trustee or court may scrutinise proofs of debt, including underlying transactions, and may admit only debts that are genuinely due, taking into account limitation rules and statutory exceptions for fraudulent breaches of trust.
Party_Details.Application: A company's assignee submitted proofs of debt against a bankrupt former director, alleging she transferred company funds to related entities and personal accounts without commercial justification. The legal conflict involves whether these alleged breaches of duty can be quantified and admitted as provable debts under bankruptcy law.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The judge first addressed whether claims based on alleged breaches of fiduciary duty by a director are provable in bankruptcy. Relying on the earlier decision in Re Medora Xerxes Jamshid, the court held that such claims are provable as unliquidated claims arising by reason of breach of trust under section 87(3) of the Bankruptcy Act, given the analogy between fiduciary and trust relationships and the policy of giving a bankrupt a fresh start by allowing a broad range of claims to be resolved in bankruptcy. The court noted that such claims are not liquidated debts and are not provable as obligations to make restitution or as contractual claims, but may be resolved through the proof of debt regime where the complexity of the issues permits. Both Fulcrum Distressed Partners Limited and the private trustee accepted that the claims could be dealt with in this way.

On the TL Sum, the court examined whether the 2007 payments from Timor Global Pte Ltd to Timor Global (TL) Pte Ltd were simply a routing of Intraco Trading Pte Ltd's advances under the 2007 joint venture agreement. The judge analysed the sequence and amounts of incoming payments from Intraco and outgoing payments to Timor Global (TL) Pte Ltd and found that the timing and quantum did not align in a way that clearly showed a conduit arrangement. Some Intraco payments were not matched by corresponding outgoing payments, and the amounts did not match exactly. The court also relied on the accounting treatment: the payments were recorded as receivables due from Timor Global (TL) Pte Ltd, and in examination proceedings Jannie Chan Siew Lee described the S$15.34 million as loans extended by Timor Global Pte Ltd to Timor Global (TL) Pte Ltd, interest-free and repayable on demand. The judge concluded that the TL Sum represented debts due from Timor Global (TL) Pte Ltd to Timor Global Pte Ltd, not merely pass-through payments under the joint venture.

The court then considered whether the director's conduct in relation to the TL Sum amounted to a breach of fiduciary duty. Applying the framework for the duty to act bona fide in the interests of the company and the creditor-focused duty when insolvency is imminent or inevitable, as set out in Goh Chan Peng, Progen, and Foo Kian Beng, the judge assessed Timor Global Pte Ltd's financial position. The company was balance sheet solvent in 2005–2007 but heavily dependent on receivables from Timor Global (TL) Pte Ltd, which were not being collected and had no repayment schedule. The liquidators' view that the company was in parlous financial straits and effectively insolvent from 2008 onwards was accepted. The judge categorised the company as in an intermediate financial stage in 2006–2007 and in a stage where insolvency proceedings were inevitable from 2008 onwards. The court found that there was no identified commercial benefit to Timor Global Pte Ltd from extending large sums to Timor Global (TL) Pte Ltd, that Timor Global (TL) Pte Ltd was not a subsidiary, and that there was no repayment schedule or demand for repayment. The director had signed off on the financial statements and acknowledged the receivables in examination proceedings. The judge concluded that causing the payments to be made, failing to pursue repayment over many years, and later approving the write-off of the receivables in 2015 in the context of impending insolvency were not consistent with acting in the interests of the company or its creditors. The court therefore held that there was a breach of the duty to act bona fide in the interests of the company in relation to the TL Sum.

On limitation, the judge considered section 6 of the Limitation Act and the case law on when a cause of action for breach of director's duty accrues, including Ho Pak Kim Realty Co Pte Ltd v Ho Soo Fong and Foo Kian Beng. The court adopted the approach that time runs from the point of breach but held that section 22(1)(a) of the Limitation Act applied. Referring to Panweld Trading Pte Ltd v Yong Kheng Leong and Dynasty Line Ltd v Sukamto Sia, the judge treated the director as a trustee-like figure for company assets and applied the rule that no limitation period applies to actions in respect of fraudulent breaches of trust. The court reasoned that extending large sums to a related company without commercial benefit, not seeking repayment, and writing off the receivables in the face of insolvency could not be reconciled with an honest belief that the conduct was in the company's interests, and therefore amounted to a fraudulent breach of trust for limitation purposes. As a result, the TL Sum claim was not time-barred and was admitted.

For the Finished Goods Sum, the court examined the 2007 joint venture agreement, which contemplated that Timor Global Pte Ltd would receive sale proceeds from its customers. The evidence showed that between June and November 2008, end-buyers paid S$3,161,247.39 directly to Timor Global (TL) Pte Ltd, and these receipts were recorded in Timor Global (TL) Pte Ltd's accounts. The private trustee had rejected this component on the basis that there was no proof that the director knew of the diversion. The judge considered the director's role and knowledge, noting that she had managed Timor Global Pte Ltd from incorporation, was its sole shareholder, signed its financial statements, and was an authorised bank signatory. The court compared the situation to Dynasty Line and Ho Soo Fong, where directors were found to have knowledge or to be under a duty to inquire given their positions and the size of the transactions. Given the magnitude of the Finished Goods Sum relative to Timor Global Pte Ltd's revenue and deficit in 2008, the judge found it implausible that the director would be unaware of the absence of these receipts and concluded that she either knew or ought to have known of the diversion. In the context of insolvency, allowing sale proceeds to be paid to another entity and not pursuing recovery was found inconsistent with the interests of creditors. The court treated this as a breach of the duty to act in the company's interests and, for limitation purposes, as a fraudulent breach of trust under section 22(1)(a), so that the six-year limitation period did not apply. The Finished Goods Sum was therefore admitted.

Regarding the Jannie Sum, the judge reviewed contemporaneous letters dated 31 December 2008, signed in the name of Jannie Chan Siew Lee, in which she confirmed that she was assuming specific debts owed by named customers to Timor Global Pte Ltd. These letters were supported by general journal entries reclassifying receivables from those customers as amounts due from her. The court rejected her later assertion that the signatures were digital and that the customers owed her personally, noting that the signatures varied and appeared to be wet-ink, that the accounting records treated the sums as company receivables, and that her alternative explanation was raised late and unsupported by documentation. The judge concluded that she had taken over the benefit of receivables that had been company assets without showing a corresponding obligation from the company to her, and that this conduct occurred when the company was insolvent. The court held that this was a misapplication of company assets and a breach of custodial and fiduciary duties, and that the claim fell within the fraudulent breach of trust exception to limitation.

For the Purported Repayment, the court noted the August 2007 transfer of S$1,276,800 from Timor Global Pte Ltd's bank account to the director, recorded as a loan repayment. The director did not dispute receiving the funds but claimed they repaid a loan she had made to the company. The judge found no documentary evidence of such a loan and no entries in the company's records supporting a liability of that amount to her. In light of the company's financial position and the absence of supporting documentation, the court accepted the private trustee's view that the transfer represented a diversion of company funds to the director without justification. The judge treated this as a breach of fiduciary duty and a fraudulent breach of trust for limitation purposes, so that section 22(1)(a) of the Limitation Act applied and the claim was not time-barred.

Overall, the court held that the TL Sum and the Finished Goods Sum were provable debts arising from breaches of fiduciary duty and should be admitted, reversing the private trustee's rejection of those components. The court upheld the trustee's admission of the Jannie Sum and the Purported Repayment, rejecting the bankrupt's challenges. The judge emphasised that the trustee had a duty to scrutinise proofs of debt but that, on the evidence, the challenged components represented genuine claims against the bankrupt's estate based on her conduct as director of Timor Global Pte Ltd.

Metadata.Case_Number: [2025] SGHC 27; Bankruptcy No 2648 of 2018; Summonses Nos 4314, 4315, 4316 of 2022; Originating Application No 797 of 2022
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Jannie Chan Siew Lee

Facts.Text: In 2012, JASC Pte Ltd obtained a loan from SME Care Pte Ltd; Jannie Chan Siew Lee was the sole director and shareholder of JASC Pte Ltd.
From 16 February 2005, Jannie Chan Siew Lee served as a director of Timor Global Pte Ltd.
From its incorporation, Jannie Chan Siew Lee was the sole shareholder of Timor Global Pte Ltd.
Jannie Chan Siew Lee was a 70% shareholder of Timor Global (TL) Pte Ltd, a company registered in Timor Leste.
From its incorporation until its entry into liquidation, Jannie Chan Siew Lee was a director of Timor Global Pte Ltd.
In 2012, JASC Pte Ltd obtained a loan from SME Care Pte Ltd, secured by mortgages and a personal guarantee by Jannie Chan Siew Lee.
In 2014, JASC Pte Ltd had not fully repaid the loan principal, late interest, late payment processing fees and extension fees owed to SME Care Pte Ltd.
In August 2007, S$1,276,800 was transferred from Timor Global Pte Ltd's bank account to Jannie Chan Siew Lee and recorded as a loan repayment.
On 31 December 2008, letters and journal entries recorded that Jannie Chan Siew Lee was assuming debts owed by named customers to Timor Global Pte Ltd.
On 31 December 2008, a letter signed by Jannie Chan Siew Lee confirmed that she was assuming debts of US$16,422.09 owed by David G. Brice to Timor Global Pte Ltd.

In 2013, during the EJD Proceedings, Jannie Chan Siew Lee stated that Timor Global Pte Ltd had lent Timor Global (TL) Pte Ltd various monies over the years and that the total amount owing from TL was S$15,343,006.93; she also described the loans as cash loans, secured by a director, interest free and repayable on demand.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a bankrupt's challenges to the admission of proofs of debt—stemming from a consent judgment and alleged directorial breaches of trust—are legally viable.
Party_Details.Rule: Upon a bankruptcy order, the bankrupt's property vests in the trustee, who must examine proofs of debt and admit only debts that are genuinely due, applying the statutory framework for provable debts and limitation. The trustee and the court may go behind judgments and settlements where there is a reasonable basis to suspect that the underlying debts are not genuine, but generally will not do so absent indications of fraud, collusion or miscarriage of justice. Claims based on director conduct may be treated as unliquidated claims arising from of trust and are limitation rules, with exceptions for fraudulent breaches of trust.
Party_Details.Application: The bankrupt challenged the admission of debts claimed by a former lender and the assignee of her former company. The dispute assesses whether there is sufficient evidence of fraud, collusion, or misapplication of company assets to warrant going behind a consent judgment or rejecting the assignee's claims of fiduciary breaches.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The judge considered three main applications involving the bankrupt, Jannie Chan Siew Lee: her application to set aside SME Care Pte Ltd's proof of debt, the assignee's application to reverse the private trustee's rejection of parts of Timor Global Pte Ltd's proof of debt, and her application to challenge the trustee's admission of other parts of that proof. On the SME Care Pte Ltd proof, the court noted that the proof was based on a consent judgment entered on 7 May 2018 for $2,150,000 plus contractual interest. The judge reviewed the principles governing when a trustee or court may go behind a judgment in the proof of debt context, including the need for indications of fraud, collusion or miscarriage of justice, and the requirement that a consent judgment generally be set aside only in separate proceedings. The court observed that earlier litigation (SME Care Pte Ltd v Chan Siew Lee Jannie [2018] SGHC 96) had already addressed and rejected allegations of fraud or misrepresentation in relation to the settlement agreement. In the present proceedings, the bankrupt's further assertions about pressure, lack of legal representation and not reading the documents were found not to amount to evidence of fraud, undue influence or misrepresentation. The judge also noted that the private trustee had in fact examined the underlying loan, the interest rates and the moneylending transaction, and had adjusted the interest calculation from compound to simple interest. The court concluded that there was no basis to go behind the consent judgment further and no basis to invalidate SME Care Pte Ltd's admitted claim, and therefore dismissed the bankrupt's application to reverse the trustee's partial admission.

On the Timor Global Pte Ltd proof of debt, the judge first determined that claims based on alleged breaches of fiduciary duty by a director are provable in bankruptcy as unliquidated claims arising by reason of breach of trust under section 87(3) of the Bankruptcy Act, following Re Medora Xerxes Jamshid. The court then examined the TL Sum, the Finished Goods Sum, the Jannie Sum and the Purported Repayment. For the TL Sum, the judge analysed the 2007 joint venture agreement with Intraco Trading Pte Ltd and Timor Global (TL) Pte Ltd and the pattern of payments between Intraco, Timor Global Pte Ltd and Timor Global (TL) Pte Ltd. The court found that the timing and amounts of the 2007 incoming and outgoing payments did not align in a way that clearly showed that Timor Global Pte Ltd was merely a conduit for Intraco's advances. The accounting treatment of the payments as receivables due from Timor Global (TL) Pte Ltd and the bankrupt's own description of them as loans in examination proceedings supported the conclusion that the TL Sum represented debts owed by Timor Global (TL) Pte Ltd to Timor Global Pte Ltd. The judge then applied the framework for the duty to act bona fide in the interests of the company and the creditor-focused duty when insolvency is imminent or inevitable, as set out in Goh Chan Peng, Progen and Foo Kian Beng. The court accepted the liquidators' assessment that Timor Global Pte Ltd was in financial difficulty from 2005–2007 and insolvent from 2008 onwards, with its largest asset category being receivables from Timor Global (TL) Pte Ltd that were not being collected. The judge concluded that causing large payments to be made to Timor Global (TL) Pte Ltd without identified commercial benefit, not seeking repayment over many years, and approving the write-off of the receivables in 2015 in the context of impending insolvency were inconsistent with acting in the interests of the company or its creditors and amounted to breaches of fiduciary duty.

On limitation, the court considered section 6 of the Limitation Act and the case law on when a cause of action for breach of director's duty accrues, including Ho Pak Kim Realty Co Pte Ltd v Ho Soo Fong and Foo Kian Beng. The judge adopted the approach that time runs from the point of breach but held that section 22(1)(a) of the Limitation Act applied. Referring to Panweld Trading Pte Ltd v Yong Kheng Leong and Dynasty Line Ltd v Sukamto Sia, the court treated the director as a trustee-like figure for company assets and applied the rule that no limitation period applies to actions in respect of fraudulent breaches of trust. The judge reasoned that extending large sums to a related company without commercial benefit, not seeking repayment, and writing off the receivables in the face of insolvency could not be reconciled with an honest belief that the conduct was in the company's interests, and therefore amounted to a fraudulent breach of trust for limitation purposes. As a result, the TL Sum claim was not time-barred and was admitted.

For the Finished Goods Sum, the court examined the 2007 joint venture agreement, which contemplated that Timor Global Pte Ltd would receive sale proceeds from its customers. The evidence showed that between June and November 2008, end-buyers paid S$3,161,247.39 directly to Timor Global (TL) Pte Ltd, and these receipts were recorded in Timor Global (TL) Pte Ltd's accounts. The private trustee had rejected this component on the basis that there was no proof that the bankrupt knew of the diversion. The judge considered her role and knowledge, noting that she had managed Timor Global Pte Ltd from incorporation, was its sole shareholder, signed its financial statements, and was an authorised bank signatory. The court compared the situation to Dynasty Line and Ho Soo Fong, where directors were found to have knowledge or to be under a duty to inquire given their positions and the size of the transactions. Given the magnitude of the Finished Goods Sum relative to Timor Global Pte Ltd's revenue and deficit in 2008, the judge found it implausible that she would be unaware of the absence of these receipts and concluded that she either knew or ought to have known of the diversion. In the context of insolvency, allowing sale proceeds to be paid to another entity and not pursuing recovery was found inconsistent with the interests of creditors. The court treated this as a breach of the duty to act in the company's interests and, for limitation purposes, as a fraudulent breach of trust under section 22(1)(a), so that the six-year limitation period did not apply. The Finished Goods Sum was therefore admitted.

Regarding the Jannie Sum, the judge reviewed contemporaneous letters dated 31 December 2008, signed in the name of the bankrupt, in which she confirmed that she was assuming specific debts owed by named customers to Timor Global Pte Ltd. These letters were supported by general journal entries reclassifying receivables from those customers as amounts due from her. The court rejected her later assertion that the signatures were digital and that the customers owed her personally, noting that the signatures varied and appeared to be wet-ink, that the accounting records treated the sums as company receivables, and that her alternative explanation was raised late and unsupported by documentation. The judge concluded that she had taken over the benefit of receivables that had been company assets without showing a corresponding obligation from the company to her, and that this conduct occurred when the company was insolvent. The court held that this was a misapplication of company assets and a breach of custodial and fiduciary duties, and that the claim fell within the fraudulent breach of trust exception to limitation.

For the Purported Repayment, the court noted the August 2007 transfer of S$1,276,800 from Timor Global Pte Ltd's bank account to the bankrupt, recorded as a loan repayment. She did not dispute receiving the funds but claimed they repaid a loan she had made to the company. The judge found no documentary evidence of such a loan and no entries in the company's records supporting a liability of that amount to her. In light of the company's financial position and the absence of supporting documentation, the court accepted the private trustee's view that the transfer represented a diversion of company funds to her without justification. The judge treated this as a breach of fiduciary duty and a fraudulent breach of trust for limitation purposes, so that section 22(1)(a) of the Limitation Act applied and the claim was not time-barred.

The court therefore dismissed the bankrupt's applications to overturn the trustee's admissions of SME Care Pte Ltd's proof of debt and of the Jannie Sum and Purported Repayment, and allowed the assignee's applications to admit the TL Sum and the Finished Goods Sum as provable debts in her bankruptcy.
